pick.rationale: Despite nearly identical KenPom ratings (+25.19 vs +25.64), Texas Tech holds a meaningful Torvik edge (#12 vs #17) driven by an elite defense ranked 27th nationally — a profile perfectly positioned to neutralize Alabama's offense-first identity and exploit the Crimson Tide's defense ranked only 67th nationally. The decisive context tipping this matchup to the Red Raiders is Alabama's loss of Aden Holloway, their No. 2 scorer and top three-point shooter, who was arrested on felony drug charges on March 16 and removed from campus just days before the tournament, severely depleting Alabama's perimeter scoring depth. Texas Tech's own wound — the season-ending ACL tear suffered by JT Toppin (21.8 PPG) in February — is real and explains their three-game losing skid, but their defensive system remains intact under Grant McCasland and Christian Anderson is confirmed healthy. The matchup asymmetry — Texas Tech's top-27 defense against a now-shorthanded Alabama offense, and Texas Tech's top-9 offense against Alabama's leaky 67th-ranked defense — makes the Red Raiders the right side in what is otherwise a near-coin-flip on raw efficiency numbers.

reasoningStep.title: Texas Tech vs Alabama — Round of 32 Matchup Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Both teams enter with significant personnel losses, but the matchup dynamics and injury context favor Texas Tech. Alabama's Aden Holloway (No. 2 scorer, top 3-point shooter) was arrested on felony charges March 16 and is almost certainly unavailable, while Texas Tech's JT Toppin (21.8 PPG) suffered a season-ending ACL tear in February. Texas Tech's elite defense (Torvik #12, AdjDE 98.77, 27th nationally) is a severe matchup problem for an Alabama team now operating without its best perimeter shooter, and Alabama's defense (67th nationally, AdjDE 102.77) is a liability against Texas Tech's top-9 offense. The Torvik gap (#12 vs #17) is larger than KenPom suggests and reflects Texas Tech's defensive identity, which is historically well-suited to tournament play.
evidence: Texas Tech: KenPom #19 (AdjEM +25.19), Torvik #12 (AdjOE 126.3 rank 9th, AdjDE 98.77 rank 27th). Alabama: KenPom #16 (AdjEM +25.64), Torvik #17 (AdjOE 129.4 rank 3rd, AdjDE 102.77 rank 67th).

Alabama guard Aden Holloway (No. 2 scorer, top 3-point shooter) arrested on felony drug charges March 16, removed from campus, status for tournament almost certainly unavailable per multiple reports including NBC News and AP.

JT Toppin (21.8 PPG, AP preseason All-American) suffered season-ending ACL tear Feb. 17. Texas Tech lost 3 straight entering tournament. Christian Anderson (groin) confirmed available for NCAA Tournament.
Texas Tech's top-27 defense vs. Alabama's now-depleted offense without Holloway is the decisive matchup asymmetry. Alabama's 67th-ranked defense is a liability against Texas Tech's top-9 offense.
Alabama entered tournament after early SEC Tournament exit with low spirits; Texas Tech's Big 12 battle-testing and defensive system remain intact despite Toppin loss.

result.text: Could Texas Tech basketball be on upset alert in March Madness? Skip to main content Home High School Red Raiders SportsbookWire MMA Junkie Golf Week Is Texas Tech basketball on upset alert in March Madness? Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal Grant McCasland knows that his Texas Tech basketball team is ripe for the upset pick in the 2026 edition of March Madness. The Red Raiders were once one of the top teams in the country, but the season-ending injury to JT Toppin caused some shuffling around with the team, which will enter the NCAA Tournament having lost three straight and struggled to stop anybody on the defensive end or get physical enough on the boards to compete at a high level. Think you can beat our expert? Join USA TODAY’s Bracket Challenge today! Their first-round opponent, meanwhile, is one of the hottest teams in the country and features a robust offensive arsenal that would put any team it faces in danger. Akron 's only loss in the last 20 games was against Miami (Ohio) and the Zips ran through the MAC tournament to get to the Big Dance. With everything on the line from here on out, is Texas Tech in position to be another 5-seed casualty? Ready to win March Madness? Join USA TODAY’s Survivor Pool today! March Madness upset prediction for Texas Tech basketball In the first run through of breaking down the game , I had Akron taking the 84-80 win over Texas Tech. Examining things a bit further, there's not a ton to sway our minds other than Akron not having played a ton of power-conference competition. Cut the Zips some slack here as higher-level teams rarely want to play a legitimately good mid-major for fear of losing. Akron fits that bill having made the NCAA Tournament each of the past three years and four times in the last five years. Akron's only major-conference competition this year was Purdue, which beat the Zips 97-79 at home back in November. That game was five days before the Boilermakers hammered Texas Tech 86-56 in the Bahamas. Many of the players on Akron's roster have NCAA Tournament experience, something Texas Tech doesn't have the luxury of this time around. Only Luke Bamgboye had March Madness experience of the transfers brought in from the portal while just Christian Anderson and Toppin (who won't be a factor this year) played significant minutes in the tournament before this season among the returners. Buy Texas Tech NCAA Tournament tickets at StubHub Texas Tech's other three starters — Donovan Atwell, Jaylen Petty and LeJuan Watts — will all be making their first appearances in the NCAA Tournament. How will the new faces handle the pressure of trying to keep Texas Tech's hopes of a deep run going? Texas Tech's NCAA Tournament path is not the easiest Last year's Red Raiders had the benefit of playing three straight double-digit seeds before the Elite Eight against top-seeded and eventual national champion Florida. Rick Scuteri / AP By Justin Williams Feb. 18, 2026 Share full article 22 Texas Tech star forward JT Toppin suffered a season-ending knee injury in Tuesday’s 72-67 road loss to Arizona State , the university announced. An MRI confirmed a torn ACL in Toppin’s right knee. The reigning Big 12 Player of the Year exited late in the second half of Tuesday’s game after falling to the ground on a fast break, clutching his right leg. He had to be helped off the floor and was taken to the locker room soon after. Advertisement The 6-foot-8 junior is the team’s leading scorer and rebounder this season, averaging 21.8 points and 10.8 rebounds. He had 20 points and eight rebounds when he left the game against Arizona State, with his team down 61-56. It was Toppin’s 15th 20-point game of the season. The No. 13 Red Raiders dropped to 19-7 overall and 9-4 in Big 12 play, falling to fifth in the league standings. Now, the far greater concern is how the team will fare without its best player for the remainder of a season that had national championship hopes, on the heels of last season’s Elite Eight run. result.text: Can Alabama basketball survive March Madness without Aden Holloway? Skip to main content Home TuscPreps College Columns TideSports.com SportsbookWire MMA Junkie Golf Week What does Alabama basketball look like without Aden Holloway in March Madness? Emilee Smarr Tuscaloosa News Spirits were low for Alabama basketball coming into the NCAA Tournament after an early exit from the SEC tourney in Nashville. Now, the Crimson Tide carries more heartbreak to Tampa as it stares down March Madness without its top three-point shooter. On Monday, March 16, Alabama guard Aden Holloway was arrested for first-degree possession of marijuana and failure to affix a tax stamp and was transported to the Tuscaloosa County Jail. The University of Alabama has yet to release a statement on Holloway, or his status. However, facing two felony charges, it is hard to see a world in which the junior returns to competition. Not with the Crimson Tide, but possibly ever again. REQUIRED READING: What failure to affix tax stamp means for Alabama basketball's Aden Holloway GOODBREAD: Why Alabama's Nate Oats should suspend Aden Holloway Holloway's decisions have impacted more than just himself, though. The question is no longer whether Alabama can win a national championship in the face of adversity. Instead, it's if the Crimson Tide can survive even one game without one of the anchors to its nationally-acclaimed backcourt. Prior to his arrest, Holloway was averaging 16.8 points per game. Converting nearly 44% of attempts, he was the nation's 11th best shooter from the arc and ranked third in the SEC. He shot 48% from the field and also had 3.8 assists per game. Alabama has competed without Holloway before — as he dealt with a nagging wrist injury throughout non-conference play. Considering the Crimson Tide's strength-of-schedule, the fact that Alabama has done it without Holloway at 100% could suggest that it isn't time to panic. Well, March is not December. Holloway missed the season opener against North Dakota. He also was sidelined for the SEC/ACC Challenge against Clemson and the next game against UTSA. Arizona marked the Auburn transfer's return, but the 96-75 loss also gave an idea of what Alabama looks like on a Holloway off-day as he went scoreless over 21 minutes. A complete domination of UTSA also made it easier to forget how the Crimson Tide crumbled in the second half against the Tigers. Holloway hasn't missed a game since Dec. 13, and Alabama couldn't have afforded for him to be on the bench against Texas A&M or on the road at Neville Arena. In those games, the 6-foot-1 downhill threat delivered critical buckets late. As of late, he's also emerged as a key rebounder and defender for the Crimson Tide. With or without Holloway, defense has been a problem. Offensively, Alabama doesn't have the depth from the perimeter that Crimson Tide coach Nate Oats had hoped for in the preseason. Alabama finds more ways to score around the rim and from the one-line nowadays. Still, th

result.text: Could Texas Tech basketball be on upset alert in March Madness? Skip to main content Home High School Red Raiders SportsbookWire MMA Junkie Golf Week Is Texas Tech basketball on upset alert in March Madness? Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal Grant McCasland knows that his Texas Tech basketball team is ripe for the upset pick in the 2026 edition of March Madness. The Red Raiders were once one of the top teams in the country, but the season-ending injury to JT Toppin caused some shuffling around with the team, which will enter the NCAA Tournament having lost three straight and struggled to stop anybody on the defensive end or get physical enough on the boards to compete at a high level. Think you can beat our expert? Join USA TODAY’s Bracket Challenge today! Their first-round opponent, meanwhile, is one of the hottest teams in the country and features a robust offensive arsenal that would put any team it faces in danger. Akron 's only loss in the last 20 games was against Miami (Ohio) and the Zips ran through the MAC tournament to get to the Big Dance. With everything on the line from here on out, is Texas Tech in position to be another 5-seed casualty? Ready to win March Madness? Join USA TODAY’s Survivor Pool today! March Madness upset prediction for Texas Tech basketball In the first run through of breaking down the game , I had Akron taking the 84-80 win over Texas Tech. Examining things a bit further, there's not a ton to sway our minds other than Akron not having played a ton of power-conference competition. Cut the Zips some slack here as higher-level teams rarely want to play a legitimately good mid-major for fear of losing. Akron fits that bill having made the NCAA Tournament each of the past three years and four times in the last five years. Akron's only major-conference competition this year was Purdue, which beat the Zips 97-79 at home back in November. That game was five days before the Boilermakers hammered Texas Tech 86-56 in the Bahamas. Many of the players on Akron's roster have NCAA Tournament experience, something Texas Tech doesn't have the luxury of this time around. Only Luke Bamgboye had March Madness experience of the transfers brought in from the portal while just Christian Anderson and Toppin (who won't be a factor this year) played significant minutes in the tournament before this season among the returners. Buy Texas Tech NCAA Tournament tickets at StubHub Texas Tech's other three starters — Donovan Atwell, Jaylen Petty and LeJuan Watts — will all be making their first appearances in the NCAA Tournament. How will the new faces handle the pressure of trying to keep Texas Tech's hopes of a deep run going? Texas Tech's NCAA Tournament path is not the easiest Last year's Red Raiders had the benefit of playing three straight double-digit seeds before the Elite Eight against top-seeded and eventual national champion Florida. Rick Scuteri / AP By Justin Williams Feb. 18, 2026 Share full article 22 Texas Tech star forward JT Toppin suffered a season-ending knee injury in Tuesday’s 72-67 road loss to Arizona State , the university announced. An MRI confirmed a torn ACL in Toppin’s right knee. The reigning Big 12 Player of the Year exited late in the second half of Tuesday’s game after falling to the ground on a fast break, clutching his right leg. He had to be helped off the floor and was taken to the locker room soon after. Advertisement The 6-foot-8 junior is the team’s leading scorer and rebounder this season, averaging 21.8 points and 10.8 rebounds. He had 20 points and eight rebounds when he left the game against Arizona State, with his team down 61-56. It was Toppin’s 15th 20-point game of the season. The No. 13 Red Raiders dropped to 19-7 overall and 9-4 in Big 12 play, falling to fifth in the league standings. Now, the far greater concern is how the team will fare without its best player for the remainder of a season that had national championship hopes, on the heels of last season’s Elite Eight run. Toppin is in his second season with Texas

Can Alabama basketball survive March Madness without Aden Holloway? Skip to main content Home TuscPreps College Columns TideSports.com SportsbookWire MMA Junkie Golf Week What does Alabama basketball look like without Aden Holloway in March Madness? Emilee Smarr Tuscaloosa News Spirits were low for Alabama basketball coming into the NCAA Tournament after an early exit from the SEC tourney in Nashville. Now, the Crimson Tide carries more heartbreak to Tampa as it stares down March Madness without its top three-point shooter. On Monday, March 16, Alabama guard Aden Holloway was arrested for first-degree possession of marijuana and failure to affix a tax stamp and was transported to the Tuscaloosa County Jail. The University of Alabama has yet to release a statement on Holloway, or his status. However, facing two felony charges, it is hard to see a world in which the junior returns to competition. Not with the Crimson Tide, but possibly ever again. REQUIRED READING: What failure to affix tax stamp means for Alabama basketball's Aden Holloway GOODBREAD: Why Alabama's Nate Oats should suspend Aden Holloway Holloway's decisions have impacted more than just himself, though. The question is no longer whether Alabama can win a national championship in the face of adversity. Instead, it's if the Crimson Tide can survive even one game without one of the anchors to its nationally-acclaimed backcourt. Prior to his arrest, Holloway was averaging 16.8 points per game. Converting nearly 44% of attempts, he was the nation's 11th best shooter from the arc and ranked third in the SEC. He shot 48% from the field and also had 3.8 assists per game. Alabama has competed without Holloway before — as he dealt with a nagging wrist injury throughout non-conference play. Considering the Crimson Tide's strength-of-schedule, the fact that Alabama has done it without Holloway at 100% could suggest that it isn't time to panic. Well, March is not December. Holloway missed the season opener against North Dakota. He also was sidelined for the SEC/ACC Challenge against Clemson and the next game against UTSA. Arizona marked the Auburn transfer's return, but the 96-75 loss also gave an idea of what Alabama looks like on a Holloway off-day as he went scoreless over 21 minutes. A complete domination of UTSA also made it easier to forget how the Crimson Tide crumbled in the second half against the Tigers. Holloway hasn't missed a game since Dec. 13, and Alabama couldn't have afforded for him to be on the bench against Texas A&M or on the road at Neville Arena. In those games, the 6-foot-1 downhill threat delivered critical buckets late. As of late, he's also emerged as a key rebounder and defender for the Crimson Tide. With or without Holloway, defense has been a problem. Offensively, Alabama doesn't have the depth from the perimeter that Crimson Tide coach Nate Oats had hoped for in the preseason. modelTrace.content:
I now have a comprehensive picture. Let me synthesize all the evidence:

**Key findings:**

**Texas Tech:**
- KenPom #19 (AdjEM +25.19), Torvik #12 — notably higher Torvik ranking
- Elite defense: AdjDE 98.77 (27th nationally by Torvik), top-9 offense (AdjOE 126.3)
- **JT Toppin (21.8 PPG, AP preseason All-American) — season-ending ACL tear Feb. 17**
- Lost 3 straight entering the tournament
- Christian Anderson (groin injury) confirmed available
- 22-10 record, Big 12 battle-tested

**Alabama:**
- KenPom #16 (AdjEM +25.64), Torvik #17
- 3rd-best offense nationally (AdjOE 129.4), but defense ranked 67th (AdjDE 102.77)
- **Aden Holloway (No. 2 scorer, top 3-point shooter) — arrested on felony drug charges March 16, removed from campus, almost certainly unavailable**
- Early exit from SEC Tournament
- 23-9 record, SEC-tested

**Matchup dynamics:**
- On paper, Alabama has a slight KenPom edge (+25.64 vs +25.19) but Texas Tech has a larger Torvik edge (#12 vs #17)
- Texas Tech's defense (27th nationally) is a massive advantage against Alabama's offense-dependent profile
- Alabama's defense (67th nationally) is a severe liability against Texas Tech's top-9 offense
- Both teams have significant injury/availability concerns: Texas Tech lost Toppin (star forward), Alabama lost Holloway (No. 2 scorer)
- Texas Tech's defensive identity under Grant McCasland is better suited to tournament play
- The Torvik gap favoring Texas Tech (+12 vs +17) is meaningful and reflects the defensive edge
- Alabama's 3-point shooting is now severely depleted without Holloway, their top shooter

The matchup strongly favors Texas Tech: their elite defense neutralizes Alabama's offense-first identity, and Alabama's own defensive weakness (67th nationally) is exposed by Texas Tech's top-9 offense. The Holloway arrest/suspension is a critical blow to Alabama's scoring depth. Texas Tech's defensive profile is historically well-suited to tournament play, and while the Toppin loss is real, the Red Raiders' system-based defense remains intact.
